multiplechoiceresponse.label: Why does Lecture 2 describe ε-demographic parity as allowing a tunable trade-off?

choicegroup.choice: Because ε sets a tolerance on how much individual risk scores may be adjusted
Because ε determines how strongly protected attributes influence predictions
Because smaller ε enforces stricter parity at potentially higher cost to meritocracy
Because ε directly controls the model’s overall classification accuracy

div.p: ε acts as a policy lever that controls how strictly the model must equalize positive prediction rates across groups. Smaller ε values impose stricter parity, often requiring more outcome flips and thus potentially reducing merit-based distinctions. Larger ε relaxes the fairness constraint, preserving more of the original predictions. This makes ε a tunable parameter for explicitly balancing fairness and performance.
